desc:
This small brook flows into the plains here, from the forest to the east. The
settlers to the north evidently appreciate the constant water source for there
are signs everywhere of people and animals coming to the brook for its supply of
fresh, clear mountain water. The brook flows to the west, while lush plains
spread out to the south.
name: Brook close to the Settlement

desc:
Here, on the slopes of the Misty Mountains, a low birch forest has managed to
grow. Most of the trees are covered in lichen, starting a few feet up the
trunks. Their tiny branches create a dense patchwork of a canopy, making this a
haven for small birds. Just beneath the slopes to the west there seems to be a
camp of some kind, behind a wall of wooden stakes.
name: A Birch Forest

desc:
Except for the low, hardy bushes little can grow here, the wind and cold making
it a hostile environment to wildlife. West the slopes lessen and first brush
then trees are visible. There is a whole forest there, some rare shelter for the
vegetation underneath. The mountains provide an impassable wall not far to the
north, their towering faces of rock giving no hand-holds to climb.
name: Foothills of the Misty Mountains

desc:
Nothing much can grow here in the infertile earth, high above the plains. The
bare land slopes up towards the giant peaks, impassable obstacles with gleaming
white caps high in the clouds. A few rocks and boulders have fallen down from
the cliffs, breaking the sandy soil.
name: Southern Slopes of the Misty Mountains

desc:
A high grey wall of rock rises above you, a large spur of the Misty Mountains
reaching into the plains of Dunland. The grey granite blocks all passage north,
stretching for many miles east and west. The ground slopes slightly down to the
south-west, but the terrain does not change much from the rocky and infertile
soil here.
name: Lower Slopes of the Misty Mountains

desc:
Here, under the shadow of the towering rockface east, are some elevated and
isolated lands. A few bushes manage to grow, trying to cover the rocky ground as
they take advantage of the shelter. The grass is low and brown, conditioned by
the hard weather. East the grey stone of the Misty Mountains rises high into the
sky, blocking all further passage.
name: Rocky, Barren Lands

desc:
Thrown out from the mountains like a rippling arm in the plains, this range of
hills is no more than mere foothills to the heights you can see east of here.
Still, they take you quite a distance above the plains, giving a fair view of
the land for several miles. Climbing down to the west or north is quite tricky,
and would require care to avoid falling.
name: A Spur of Hills from the Mountains

desc:
Low bushes crawl across the wind-whipped plains. Among the shrubs are a few low
trees with bent and crooked branches, tortured by the constant wind from the
mountains. Sometimes sounds can be heard from within the bushes, an indication
of wildlife in this desolate part of Dunland. From the steep hills to the east
falls a mountain stream, which trickles its way towards a forest visible miles
to the west.
name: Windswept Plains

desc:
Thick grass seems to flourish here among the low, creeping bushes that cover the
dusty mountain soil. A few birches and pines stand lonely against the harsh
winds, the ground beneath them bare and hard-packed soil. North of here the
ground starts to slope upwards towards the Misty Mountains, providing a
seemingly easy start on an impossible journey in that direction. In amongst the
brush is a brook, twisting its way through the plains but always ending up going
westward.
name: Brushy Plains

desc:
In this mixed pine and birch forest travelling is not very easy. There is much
dense underbrush to fight, the branches a few feet above the ground. The dark
brown soil is covered with needles, leaves and a few branches, a soft lair that
makes it hard to move around silently. Barely audible is the tinkling of a line
of water, hidden in the undergrowth.
name: Dense Forest

desc:
Several different kinds of pines have managed to grow here, well nourished by
the waters of a mountain stream which passes nearby. It flows in from the east,
all the time gathering in water from small brooks, and continues north through
the trees. Traces of larger wild animals can be found scattered around - large
footprints and crushed undergrowth showing their route to the water.
name: A Mixed Birch and Pine Forest

desc:
Many birches grow here in these wooded uplands. The vegetation is sparse, mostly
consisting of moss clinging to the exposed rock and various kinds of small hardy
bushes. Most of it grows near a stream, flourishing in its clear water. There
are quite a few hunting paths, their well-worn condition showing recent use.
West of here the forest grows less dense and eventually finds itself defeated by
the plains of Dunland.
name: A Birch Forest

desc:
Birches dominate this part of the forest, before the tremendous heights just
north-east of here. The branches reach outwards from the stems a couple of feet
above the brushy ground, making it quite hard to travel. Broken twigs and all
sorts of different tracks can be found in the hard soil, with small screes a
common obstacle. Just south of here a few pines and spruces have managed to get
a foothold among the crowded birches, making movement there very tiring.
name: A Birch Forest

desc:
Sloping gently upwards to the north-east, these plains are battered constantly
by the winds which funnel down from the mountains. East the mountains edge ever
closer together, until in the distance the arc closes. Immediately west of here
lies a large forest of birches, stretching for several miles. It would seem to
be the only place around here that cover from the winds could be found.
name: Windswept Uplands

desc:
These plains are in a huge three-sided basin, surrounded by mountains on all
sides but west. Two giant spurs of mountains thrown out from the main range
create this isolation, dominating the horizon. However wildlife cannot thrive in
it, due to the winds that sweep down from the east. Funnelled by the narrow
valley where the two spurs meet, they streak down the valley almost constantly,
stripping the earth to a rocky soil.
name: High Plains

desc:
These lower slopes of the Misty Mountains are covered in low bushes and a few
sparsely placed birches. To the west and south the trees grow in larger numbers
to form a small forest. To your east the lack of taller growth allows the cold
winds to howl fiercely across the open slopes, stopping anything but the very
sturdiest of plants from surviving.
name: Bush-covered Slopes of the Misty Mountains

desc:
Tall, lush grass is the predominant vegetation on these plains, thriving in the
fertile soil and well nourished by the constant water supply to the north.
Grasslands extend further to the south and a series of uneven plains begin to
the west. A large birch and pine forest looms above you to the east.
name: Lush Plains

desc:
Sharp rocks and large boulders bulge out from the soft bed of grass, making the
ground craggy and far from comfortable for travellers. A wide range of hills
runs across the western grasslands, like an outstretched arm from the mountain.
name: Craggy Grasslands

desc:
Smooth plains thick with a healthy covering of lush green grasses and clover lie
here, making this an excellent place to graze horses and other livestock from
the settlement to the east. A waist-high, solid wooden fence surrounds all sides
of the grazing plains, with a large gate to the east. A brook borders the plains
to the south and west and a path is visible to the north.
name: Grazing Plains

desc:
Built so well it is nearly draftproof, this storage lodge holds most of the food
and skins that the settlers save up in reserve for those months in which food
might be scarce. Piles of skins line the wall up to the ceiling in the back of
the lodge and smoked meat hangs in strips from the ceiling along with strings of
dried herbs and small vegetables. A few women of the village are busy reworking
some of the skins into garments and blankets for the village and others can be
seen braiding the various herbs into strands to dry.
name: Storage Lodge

desc:
The inside of this lodge is small and rather dim, but the owners have done the
best they could to create the illusion of space with the placement of the few
simple pieces of furniture they have managed to collect. A small bed is pushed
against the wall to the right and looking under it, you can see a trundle bed
that can easily be pulled out at night for an extra sleeping place. A circle in
the middle of the room has been reserved as a place to build a cooking fire, and
above one can see a small hole near the top of the roof which draws the smoke up
and out of the lodge. Sturdy, wooden chairs surround the small oaken table.
name: Dunlender Home

desc:
Standing in the heart of the settlement, there is a definite decrease of noise
and activity. Things are much calmer here and it looks as if most of the lodges
surrounding you are probably the homes of the settlers. A path leads away from
the village towards the brook to the south, which borders the settlement. You
could enter one of the smaller lodges to the east but the size of the lodge
directly to the west catches your attention. It is much larger than any other
building you have seen in the settlement so far, and judging by how well it is
built, it is an important part of their village.
name: Dunlending Settlement

desc:
This large settlement seems to be in an ideal location, with its combination of
shelter from the forest to the east and its constant supply of water from the
brook to the south. The wooden lodges these people built for homes look solid
and snug against the elements, and the sides of the lodges are covered with
skins stretched across them to dry. There is an air of constant alertness for
enemies and predators that keeps these people looking suspiciously at your
intrusion into the safety of their village.
name: Dunlending Settlement

desc:
Surrounded by the sheer slopes of the Misty Mountains on all sides but west, you
find yourself in a small valley. It is partly covered with low bushes and filled
with fallen rocks fallen from the heights. It would be near insanity to climb
these mountains, the rocky walls stretching forever above your head. To the west
you can see the valley open into a huge basin between two spurs of the
mountains, gradually lowering in altitude as it heads out to the flat plains.
name: On the Lower Slopes of the Misty Mountains

desc:
A stone wall quite unexpectedly appears from the shadows, yet it is not a dead
end, but a sharp turn in the tunnel that continues northwards and grows into a
small room to the east. Unlike other corridors this seems to be carved by hard
work and effort from scratch, and is therefore much cruder.
name: Sharp Turn in the Tunnel

desc:
This is probably the first room built in this labyrinth of caves and tunnels,
now long abandoned and partly collapsed in one corner. The rotten remains of a
table are scattered on the floor with various rusty tools, and on the walls some
signs of faded drawings can be seen. The door that was once the entrance has
been torn off its hinges and is probably now used elsewhere.
name: Abandoned Warehouse

desc:
Several racks and poles have been erected in this eastern part of the hall,
where slaves and prisoners caught in the surrounding lands are tied up and used
as models for target practice. Receiving advice from their instructor,
undoubtedly proficient in the skills of pain and torture, the snaga leaving this
hall will know how to hit their enemy where it hurts most.
name: Hall of Arms

desc:
In this corner of the vast hall fresh warriors are taught their basic tasks,
whom they should obey and whom they should kill on sight. Part of the wall has
been scraped clean of dirt and is now decorated with a vague and barely legible
map. Where the faint drawing ends, shackles await disobedient snaga. They are
tied up here and whipped to teach others some manners and to satisfy the blood
lust of their instructor.
name: Hall of Arms

desc:
The deafening clatter of metal on metal and the thick forge smoke is the daily
working environment in this corner of the huge hall. The ever increasing ranks
of orcs need a constant supply of weapons and armour and the local blacksmith,
familiar with the harsh conditions of the battleground, makes sure no fellow orc
goes to combat bare-handed.
name: The Armoury

desc:
After the tiring, tedious and often painful lessons the snaga come here to eat,
sleep and quarrel. A huge steaming cauldron here looks like the mockery of an
altar, for some orcs just as significant in sacredness. The walls of the rooms
are covered in beds, hewn out of the rock.
name: The Eating Hall

desc:
Various goods have been piled in this room, hung on the walls or piled onto
shelves. As each war party returns from pillaging and looting, the supplies of
the merchant are refreshed, and it is here that the freshly recruited soldiers
and scouts can equip for their blood-curdling task. The desk between the
customers and the owner of the shop seems to be a line of chalk drawn on the
floor, the crossing of which would cost an intruder dearly.
name: Merchant's Corner

desc:
This is the central guardroom of the caves. Rickety wooden beds stand along the
walls and the table is littered with food and drink. As the guards are not
allowed to bring any entertainment into their quarters, most spend their spare
time elsewhere watching the torture of captured slaves and prisoners.
name: Cave of the Guards

desc:
This medium-sized cavern is home to a central guardpoint, meant to prevent any
intruder from getting any further into the caves. A grotesquely carved doorway
lies to the north, while weapon racks seem to be placed for soldiers storming in
through the southern door. The few pieces of furniture here have been positioned
to enable effortless guarding of the tunnels east, although the ramshackle
wooden tables would not prove much defence against a strong foe.
name: Central Guardhall

desc:
Stone steps once joined the lower and upper levels, but now the linking passage
consists only of a cracked and pitted slope. The lack of any stonecraft has
undoubtedly cost many orcs broken bones and wrenched limbs, for there is a good
chance of tumbling down into darkness from here.
name: Dark and Steep Passage

desc:
A rectangular chamber of cut rock, this is the sleeping chambers of the clan's
chieftain. The floor is littered with thick animal furs, some of them still with
the head attached. Some basic pieces of furniture complete the quarters, some of
obviously orkish make, others by a more skilled craftsman. Like many of the
items in these caves, they were probably looted from the Dunlendings, or from
some passing caravan.
name: A Sleeping Chamber

desc:
Scarcely decorated stone pillars have been erected on both sides of a robust
stone throne that stands in the middle of this large cavern. The uneven walls
are decorated with various colours of carpets, probably looted from vanquished
foes, and other trophies of war. West a small passageway leads into sleeping
chambers, while south a badly-carved portal leads into a spacious cavern.
name: The Chieftain's Quarters

desc:
It is impossible to tell whether these are rough stairs or just natural bumps
and depressions that allow passage between the lower and upper levels. The side
of the tunnel has been hewn and smoothed, so heavier cargoes can be hoisted down
the slope.
name: Rough Stairs

desc:
This is a central intersection of many tunnels, a well used passageway. The
clatter of shields and weapons echo from the east, with the occasional cry of
pain. The grey tiles that cover the floor are worn bare by the constant trudging
of heavy metal boots, but a vague pattern can still be made out in them, now
long defaced.
name: Intersection of Four Tunnels

desc:
This shaft has been cut at an extremely steep angle, following the line of a
natural lode of iron. A frayed rope is the only means of support for the slave
miners. Every now and then a rumbling can be heard in the tunnel, as large rocks
dislodge themselves, crashing down the shaft and out of your sight.
name: A Sunken Mining Shaft

desc:
This is a completely new tunnel dug into the mountains. It hasn't been fully
widened yet, and the floor is still covered with gravel and rubble, making
passage tiresome. Water trickles down from between iron hooks attached to the
wall, that holds a strong rope offering support. Occasional natural depressions
in the ceiling indicate that this is by no means a safe room to linger in.
name: At the Mining Face

desc:
Although only recently dug, this passage is already taken into use, its ceiling
supported with rickety wooden props. A cavity on the southern wall bears marks
of pickaxes and other tools, as if someone had tried to dig another tunnel
there, but abandoned the idea. To the west the passage continues and opens into
a larger cavern.
name: The Supported Passage

desc:
This cave was dug for the female orcs and their offspring, who are viewed by the
rest of the orcs as an unhealthy nuisance. Some robust toys are scattered about
the floor, while a large fire with a pot swinging over it functions as a
primitive kitchen. Barely recognisable scenes of battle have been scratched on
the walls with a dark-brown matter, perhaps the mother orcs trying to teach
their young.
name: Cave of the Inferior

desc:
Robust beds stand along the walls of this cavern, while a large stone rock in
the centre acts as a table. No warrior uruk with any pride would spend a moment
here - it is the sleeping quarters of the tribe's females and their children
that don't have the strength to wield a weapon yet. The reek of filth is heavy
in the air, the quarters never having been cleaned.
name: Diseased Sleeping Quarters

desc:
The tunnels meeting here are natural, perhaps cut by some underground stream
long since diverted elsewhere. Now they have become part of the warrens of
tunnels inhabited by orcs. The western branch opens into a large cavern, while
the northern one seems to be paved with small grey tiles. On the eastern wall
there is some kind of rope-mechanism for hoisting various goods up the slope, to
the upper part of the caves.
name: Intersection of the Lower Caves

desc:
A continuous breeze of fresh air from a wide aperture in the north wall keeps
this part of the caves clean of cobwebs and dust, unlike most of the areas
deeper inside. Looking out you can discern some of the terrain outside, mainly
the steep valley walls. Undoubtedly you would be able to see more if you were to
lean closer to the aperture.
name: Watchpost above the Mountaingate

desc:
A sumptuous chamber harbours the entrance gate and provides passage to a tunnel
leading east, further into the mountain. A small, broken staircase runs along
the western wall. Originally it led to a guardpost above but now it is of little
use, due to years of neglect. Protruding from the floor, a large lever sets a
stark contrast to the surrounding smooth walls and well-cut floor.
name: Chamber of the Mountaingate

desc:
A clearing has been made here through the forested valley, the woods giving way
to a broad, paved path leading up to the very face of the rock. The trail does
not end there however, for a grand gate has been crafted and set to guard an
entrance into the mountain itself. Grim carvings mar the enormous gate and the
surrounding rock, as if to give a premonition of what lies beyond.
name: Clearing before the Mountaingate

desc:
A smooth, finely wrought tunnel cuts a passage through the mountain. Due west it
widens and reaches a large chamber. A faint roaring sound echoes in the gloomy
darkness of a tunnel running east, deeper into the mountain.
name: Elaborate Tunnel

desc:
Great pillars tower before you, reaching ever upwards into the darkness above. A
roaring sound emanates from intricate shafts at the base of each pillar as fresh
air is channelled into the tunnels. The turbulent winds lash out at you and send
dust dancing through the air, making it hard for you to see clearly.
name: Pillars of Eternity

desc:
Beds of various sizes have been lined up along the walls. Although of different
shapes and sizes, they all share one property - all are considerably small. Not
even the larger of the beds would suit an average sized man. A few cradles by
the western wall tell tales of children playing here at one time, but they are
long gone now, and nothing remains save a few scattered, discarded belongings.
name: Sleeping Chambers

desc:
Several short beds have been arrayed in some form of barrack condition. The
chamber itself is rather crude and unornamented, as if to house only the less
prominent of the dwellers. A thick layer of dust has settled upon all of the
furniture, giving it a hazy grey hue.
name: Sleeping Chambers

desc:
A pale silvery light illuminates the eastern end of this narrow, domed hallway.
The western end seems less beckoning, with its roaring sounds of turmoil, but a
constant flow of fresh and invigorating air originates from it.
name: Domed Hallway

desc:
Set with large stone tables and many seats, this hall can harbour a large host
of warriors and the like. Dwarven statues with proud and stern faces stand guard
at a brightly shining door of silver in the eastern wall. The door emits faint
gleams of silvery light that beam throughout the hall, dispelling the darkness
where they fall.
name: Gathering Hall inside the Mountain

desc:
The chamber is filled with empty trays, dust-covered tables and old barrels. A
large, but long since burned out, fireplace in the centre of the chamber tells
of grand, festive meals being prepared here.
name: Chamber of Preparation and Storage

desc:
The silvery door shines with a pale light, illuminating the emptiness of this
old corridor. A dusty carpet runs through here, following it east into a large
room. Two smaller chambers lie just north and south of here.
name: Corridor of the Dwarven Lords

desc:
Wide, comfortable beds have been arranged along the walls. A supple drapery
hangs in the doorway, providing a way to seal out unwanted light or any other
interference that might disturb a much needed nights rest.
name: Master Bedroom

desc:
Faint silvery light from the western corridor falls upon a splendid throne made
out of dark, slate-blue stone. A thick, red carpet runs up to its very base,
still in good condition despite it being many years since Thrain's folk left
these halls. Lesser seats made out of the same stone rest on both sides of the
throne, and although not as grand as the throne itself any prominent Lord would
take pride in having such a seat.
name: Mountain Throne

desc:
You have come across an old weapons and armament storage. Broken hilts and old
weapon sheaths lie scattered across the floor. Spider webs cling between empty
weapon racks and a thick layer of dust has settled upon the entire chamber.
name: Dwarven Armoury

desc:
Like ripples in the plains, these hills roll out from the main range of the
Misty Mountains to the east. Although incomparable to those vast snow-tipped
peaks, they are more than substantial themselves. From here there is a good view
out over the flatlands below. North the hills continue along the base of the
Misties, while south a cave opens in the hillside.
name: Steep Hillside Rising to Join the Mountains

desc:
A pitch-black crack in the hillside serves as an entrance, hollowing out to a
wider cavern inside. Many skeletons, the bones gnawed by some ferocious beast,
lie scattered all around, some still with flaps of meat hanging on them. A trail
of gore leads through the dusty soil, into the darkest reaches of the cave.
There a pile of furs and branches have been gathered, forming a giant bed.
name: Dank Cave

desc:
A crude chamber has been recklessly wrought out of the mountain. The broken
texture of the walls shows lack of craftsmanship, but tells of great force being
used in the construction. A tunnel cuts south through the mountain like a fresh
wound and a huge hole punctures the westernmost wall.
name: Gaping Mountain-Chamber

desc:
The core of the mountain has been ripped apart to provide passage through it.
Claw-like markings mar the walls of the tunnel like lacerations, and blood in
the form of subsoil water gently trickles out of them. The smell of smoke drifts
in from the south, mixed with some other rank odour.
name: Lacerating Tunnel

desc:
A broad smoke-filled cavern harbours a large fireplace and acts as dining- hall
of sorts. A slanting passage offers access to a small chamber just over- head,
albeit it would take a good bit of scrambling to make it up there. The southern
part of the cavern narrows considerably and forms into a small opening whereas
both the northern and western parts form into tunnels.
name: Smoke-filled Cavern

desc:
A small dugout lies nestled deep inside the mountain, secluded from the rest of
mountain dwelling by its draped doorway. Large animal hides have been strewn
across the cold floor to serve as mattresses. Daunting remains of fallen prey
lie stacked in piles along the walls of the chamber, perhaps kept as trophies or
just proof of lack of tidiness.
name: Secluded Dwelling

desc:
A seemingly unfinished tunnel grinds its way west into the mountain until
finally coming to a halt. The walls are serrated by bulging protrusions and
sharp broken angles. The westernmost part of the tunnel is cluttered with rocks
and other debris from the ongoing construction work. The crude state of the
tunnel raises the question of whether it's actually intentionally wrought or
just the result of unbridled rage.
name: Serrated Tunnel

desc:
Smoke filters in from below making the air hazy and hard to breathe in this
chamber which appears to serve as a food storage. A strong scent of cooked meat
blends with the smothering smoke but doesn't help to make the air any less
suffocating. A slanting passage leads down into a broad cavern which is lit by
the flames of a large fire.
name: Smoke-filled Mountain-Chamber

desc:
The mountainside draws up into the sky near vertically, surpassing by far the
skill of even the bravest climbers. Soft, grassy fields run along the base of
the mountainside, and the grass takes on a greyish hue from the soil below,
almost as if blending in with the mountain itself.
name: Grassy Fields Nestled at the Mountainbase

desc:
A slumped landscape, marred by broken rocks and covered with tufts of thick
grass, lies at the border of gentle meadows and fields. The ominous presence of
the great mountains to the north and east becomes more and more overwhelming as
the landscape draws closer to them, their vast peaks rising above the clouds in
places. A small spur runs along the south edge of this field, small compared to
the mountains but still preventing travel in that direction.
name: Craggy Fields

desc:
The gentle fields playfully spread across to the south and west, vast open
spaces you can see for miles. The craggy peaks of the Misty Mountains and the
tiny spur of them to the north set a cold and harsh contrast to the heathery
meadows, over which the mountains tower imperiously.
name: Heathery Meadow

desc:
A range of hills rise into the southern sky, and the meadow halts promptly,
giving way to a rocky slope. Soft peat rolls out like a smothering blanket west
of here, but in the north the meadows thrive and flourish. Like a faint, grey
shadow on the horizon, a tower can be seen to the south-west.
name: Heathery Meadow

desc:
The towering mountain range to the east throws out an arm here, making a low and
rugged spur of hills. It is covered in waist-high grass, a few small shrubs and,
here and there, stands of trees. To the west there is a ruined watchtower,
overlooking the lands below. North, there are some broken plains, and foothills
below the snow-capped peaks of the Misty Mountains. South the land below this
ridge of hills is mainly rolling plains and, to the south-west wetlands. There
is some building far off to the south of here, a farm judging by the fields all
around it.
name: Hills by the Misty Mountains

desc:
Footholds and handholds are plentiful here, most of which are unstable. There
are several small ledges to rest upon, luckily so as climbing this slope is
tedious and exhausting. The view of the land below is astounding; however
soaking up the view, and resting here for long might prove fatal. Loose stones
abound, and if one of them dislodged from above you could be flattened.
name: A Rocky Cliff

desc:
The ground is covered in rocks of varying shapes and sizes, dislodged from the
cliff above. It appears to be unstable up there, with several large boulders
that could tumble down at any moment. Here and there are large impressions in
the earth, sobering reminders of the danger. The cliff itself looks climbable,
but there is a much safer passage to the west. East the Misty Mountains are
visible not too far away, an immense presence dominating the landscape.
name: Below a Rocky Cliff

desc:
A lonely, forgotten house stands in this field of overgrown wheat. Only the rock
walls and a rotten door remains. Since the wheat crop had trouble taking root,
the farm was abandoned for the better fields in the south. The soil here is just
a fine layer of dirt, with a lot of little grey rocks - not the ideal planting
conditions for any crop.
name: At an Abandoned Farmhouse

desc:
These trees are planted in such an orderly manner. Each row is perfectly
straight. The ground is covered in a rich, dark dirt with a few grey rocks
interspersed. Since the trees are planted in such wide, even rows, this grove
doesn't offer much cover from the elements. Bordering this grove to the south
and west are some farmlands, while only monotonous fields of wild wheat are to
the east.
name: An Apple Grove

desc:
Rows of various crops are planted here, which are well tended. The land between
the crops is covered in fine well-ploughed dirt, with not a weed in sight. The
field directly west of here has no signs of plant life on it, and tall hay is
visible to the south. North, through the trees an outline of an old ruin can be
seen. A farmhouse is visible to the south-west, with numerous other small
buildings around it.
name: A Crop Field

desc:
These parcels of land are about an acre in size each and grow three crops for
feeding the various farm animals. There are fields of hay, oats and barley, in
different stages of being harvested. There are several bales on the ground,
ready for storage. The main part of the farm lies west of here, the side of a
building with no door there. To the east the plains stretch until the base of
the mountains.
name: Fields of Hay, Barley and Oats

desc:
Under the tower a natural cave has been widened, and converted into sleeping
quarters. There are three beds cut from the rock walls, with thick animal furs
the only bedding. North the cave continues, narrower than here, and above a
slightly rickety wooden ladder leads to a hatch in the ceiling. Underfoot the
rock is covered with a dusty layer of soil, damp in patches where water has
seeped in from above.
name: The Sleeping Quarters

desc:
This is the northern part of a cavern; the rocky lower half that has remained
unused. The reason is the pooling water, unable to drain away here because of
the bare stone. South the cave has been made into sleeping quarters, widened
into a habitable place. Above the rock ceiling is hidden in the dark, far above
you.
name: A Filthy Cave

desc:
Surrounded by tall stone walls on three sides and the tower to the east, this
open-air pen is used to keep the inhabitants' mounts. A modicum of shelter is
provided from the elements by two wooden stalls on the southern well, where a
rusted metal feeding trough stands. On the northern side the stone wall has
crumbled over the cliff edge at one point, giving a view of the long drop down.
name: A Walled Animal Pen

desc:
The base of the tower is earthen, the soil damp from lack of light. The walls
are circular, about thirty feet in diameter, and made of large and rough stone
blocks. Around the inside edge of the tower winds a staircase of protruding
stone blocks, leading to the level above. Two doors exit from this level of the
tower: a small western door leading to an animal pen, and the larger main door
to the south, made of thick oak wood. Barely visible in the dark northern corner
is a hatch, leading down.
name: Inside a Stone Watchtower

desc:
This is the highest accessible point in the tower. Above there used to be one
more level, but it has long since crumbled away. The stair that once led up is
no longer there, and its floor is nothing but rotten wood. Even here the planks
are splintered and cracking in places, the neglect of ages apparent. However,
even from this lesser height the view is panoramic - no movement within miles
would go unnoticed by those watching from here.
name: Ruined Watchtower

desc:
This lone tree gives an excellent view over the land nearby, although there is
little to see but brush for a good distance. The branches themselves are strong
and easily bear weight, with some of the limbs looking sturdy enough to venture
out and gather some cherries.
name: Up a Cherry Tree

desc:
Tall stalks of brown grass shift unsteadily from the constant breeze that blows
through the Gap of Rohan. The sea of green and brown seems without end, the same
turf covering the plains for miles around. To the north it fades slightly away
into a richly-coloured valley, cleaving into the mountains.
name: Grassy Turf

desc:
Brown and green grasses dominate the surrounding view to the south. Much of the
same vegetation can be seen continuing to the west and east. Mountains begin to
dominate the horizon further north, a drastic end to the flat plains that make
up the Gap of Rohan. A valley, immediately north, opens up between the
mountains.
name: Opening of a Valley

desc:
The ground becomes hard and rocky here, making travel difficult. The mountains
appear to grow right out of the plains, a vivid contrast between the green
plains and the grey rock of the mountains. The route above becomes a difficult
climb, while the valley to south continues on in the distance.
name: Valley in the Mountains

desc:
There are some small plants growing on the outer edge of the cliff, brave enough
to battle the harsh environment of the mountains. The ground is solid and there
is enough room to walk side by side. The mountains that are to the west, grow
even steeper as the peaks rise higher and higher.
name: Along the Cliff

desc:
A mix of clay and mud, the earthen walls of this cave seem to have once been
smooth and unscarred. Now, in many places, they are marred with scratches of
sharp claws. Upon the ground, which is clearly made of the very same material as
the walls, one can see more scratches coupled with the occasional small feather
pressed deep into the earth. The hollow itself is no more than twelve feet wide
and from roof to ground no more than ten feet.
name: An Earthen Hollow

desc:
Atop a broad plateau, this section of the plains is somewhat barren. There are
towering mountains, which rise up to the north and west, while the land drops
away gradually to the south and east. With the run-off of water from above and
the winds that sweep across the land at this altitude, this is not the kind of
terrain where plant life can flourish easily.
name: Sloping Plains below the Mountains

desc:
The rocky soil is the host to a variety of bushes and densely growing vines,
slowing travel somewhat. To the north, the plains slope upwards and are fairly
steep, before giving way to the majestic Misty Mountains. The foothills, or
roots of these mountains, extend all the way to this section of Rohan, some
twenty miles south of Isengard itself.
name: Thick Vegetation

desc:
The southern end of the Misty Mountains are blocking the view to the north, like
a giant wall across the horizon for dozens of miles. To the northeast, the line
breaks, allowing an entrance to Nan Curunir, the valley of the wizards. The sea
of green grass continues to both the south and west, while just head-on to the
north, the slopes form an entrance to a cave, with many, heavy footprints that
lead in that direction.
name: Valley below the Mountains

desc:
The Misty Mountains that block the way to the north are a formidable array of
peaks. There appears to be no way past those heights. Further eastwards, they
lower into a series of hills, which are the entrance to the valley of Isengard.
Here, the land is quite rocky, although interspersed with clumps of growth. As
the land lowers to the south, the greenery there appears to become more
prevalent.
name: Plains beside the Mountain Spurs

desc:
At the foot of a spur of hills, it is impossible to advance to the north, west
or east, because flat rocks reach into the sky and form a nearly vertical rock
wall. Far above to the east, the heather-covered crown of Dol Baran, like an
imperious throne, watches the land. And even further to the east, lies the
impassable Misty Mountains that appear to tower into the clouds; while to the
south the rocky area ends, dropping away into the grassy flatlands of the Gap of
Rohan.
name: At the Foot of Dol Baran

desc:
Wildlife flourishes here, quickly fleeing into tall grass at the first sign of
any movement. Birds can often be seen calling out to each other, as they fly far
overhead with some hidden purpose. The plains roll on to the south and the west,
but to the north, they are abruptly halted by the southern end of the Misty
Mountains.
name: Wild Growth

desc:
There are many varieties of plant life, laying on the border lands between the
plains and the mountains here. The colour of the field has made it a perfect
hiding spot for small birds and other creatures that live in the low grasses and
brush. The vast plains continue to the south and west. To the east, the
vegetation becomes too thick to pass.
name: Fertile Field

desc:
Leaning back into the hillside of Dol Baran above, this open dale is protected
from much of the winds that sweep through the Gap of Rohan. Its steep sides are
covered in lichen, and the lower banks by several, thick, thorny bushes.
Occasional trees and tall hawthorns rise from the soft, springy turf that
populate the glen, while above on the horizon, rise first the bare hills and
then the snow-capped Misty Mountains.
name: A Sheltered Dale

desc:
Broad and hard, the ancient highway was laid down, and it has been well
maintained. Towards the north, it approaches the entrance to the valley of
Isengard, where the road and river pass into the cleft at the end of the Misty
Mountains. The arms of the mountains wrap around it like pincers, leaving only a
narrow gap of a few miles. Below, to the west, is the steep side of a dale, open
only to the south. A trail leads towards the riverside to the east.
name: Road to Isengard

desc:
This hilly range is the western spur guarding the entrance to the valley of
Isengard. Dol Baran is the last of that range with its round, imperious and
heather-covered crown. Once there was an outpost of some sort here, but it has
long since been abandoned, leaving nothing but the lichen-covered stone
foundations. Around the hill's eastern feet runs a wide highway, roughly
following the line of the river, while a steep slope of flat large rocks
descends to the west.
name: Atop Dol Baran

desc:
The brown shoulder of the road to the east slowly gives way to a sea of green
grass that is about one foot in height. A few shrubs and plants are growing
along the edge of the road, well nourished by the nearby waters of the Isen. To
the north, a secluded glen that opens up on the side of a large round hill,
which is but a spur from the vast heights of the Misty Mountains that lies to
the northwest.
name: Upland Plains

desc:
This well-maintained road runs north to south, and although it seems to be used
relatively frequently, it is still in good order. Someone has taken great care
to keep it in excellent condition for the fast transport of people and beasts.
To the west, the plains stretch for miles upon miles of sweet upland turf. To
the east, the grass is interrupted in the distance by the line of the river
Isen.
name: Road to Isengard

desc:
The road bends here along with the river, on a raised embankment that is above
any chance of flooding. Running swiftly on its stony bed, the Isen marks the
western border of Rohan. Southwards, the road and the river enter a long, but
wide valley. Northwards though, they each head towards the entrance of a large
valley.
name: Bend in the Road to Isengard

desc:
This otherwise ancient road appears to be well maintained. It continues its
smooth passage to the east and south, along the river banks of the Isen. The
plains that lie on the western side of the road seem to go on forever into the
horizon, interrupted only by the snow-capped peaks of the Misty Mountains on
their northern side.
name: The Road to Isengard

desc:
Flat for miles around, the plains are only split to the south, where there is a
ridge that rises above the road, running just east of here. The grass sways
constantly in the breezes that swirl up the valley of the Isen, creating some
ocean-like waves in the short grass.
name: Flat Grasslands

desc:
Tall stalks of yellow-brown grass sway in the wind, growing over two feet high
in places. The dense clumps of green grass to the west, mask a rocky fall into a
valley, too steep to descend. The earth appears to be soft and springy below the
verdant growth. It is well watered by underground sources from the nearby
mountains.
name: Tall Grass

desc:
This section of the well-kept road connects the valley of Isengard to the
outside world. The tracks of wheeled carts and many hoof prints are an obvious
sign of the heavy use it is put to. On the riverbank to the east, there is a
small watchtower atop a brick building, almost giving a full view over the
entrance to the valley.
name: Road to Isengard

desc:
The River Isen flows on, widening and slowing down as it leaves the valley of
Isengard and its mountain enclosure. However, at this point, the water still
flows quite swiftly. There are a lot of debris that has fallen into the river
from the rocky outcrops on its eastern side, making the water choppy. On the
western bank lies a wooden watchtower, built to survey any approach to the
valley.
name: River Isen

desc:
The river Isen bends slightly west here, cutting around the stony outcrops on
its eastern bank. The water is chilly, although the fish, which are frequently
visible below the surface, do not seem to mind. The shallower water near the
eastern bank is full of sharp rocks and slippery stones, and is probably not the
best place to leave the river.
name: River Isen

desc:
The water is still chilly, flowing directly in swift descent from the towering
Misty Mountains that lies north of here. The river loses most of its force in
the flatter lands of the Gap, but it will take many miles before it looses its
icy touch. To the north, the river narrows into a steep-sided channel, with a
watchtower visible far off in the distance, while downstreams it bends to the
west.
name: River Isen

desc:
The river flows considerably faster here, becoming very narrow as it is squeezed
between the sheer rock of the eastern bank and the steep western side. However,
it still moves at a comparatively sedate pace, compared to its mountain origins
further upstream. The current is gentle, but persuasive and moving everything
downstream unless one swims with force against it.
name: River Isen

desc:
The trail leads from the road to a short distance to the west, where there is a
watchtower standing. The base is a simple, brick building, built without much
grace. A large, wooden tower that rises thirty or so feet above the brick
building at its base, provides a good view over the entrance to Nan Curunir. The
valley lies just to the north of here, opening up beyond the arms of the
mountains that enclose the entrance.
name: Trail to the Watchtower

desc:
To the south and east, the river Isen is visible, flowing slowly through the
flat lands. One can see patches of bushes and even occasional small trees
growing here and there, but mostly the land is nothing but short, springy turf.
West lies the road that leads north into the valley of Isengard, and south
through Rohan towards Dunland.
name: Flat Riverside Plains

desc:
Although this place lies dozens of miles away from the Misty Mountains where the
Isen is born, the water still carries the cold of its icy origins. It has long
since lost the swift mountain current though, broadening and slowing down in
these flat lands. On the western bank is a wide road, which seems to follow the
line of the river.
name: River Isen

desc:
The rocky wasteland is littered with stony debris and lined with fine cracks
that tear down beneath the parched surface of the plains. The dusty landscape
continues in all directions, except to the south where a spur of hills rises
higher than the surrounding plains.
name: Rocky Plains

desc:
The sun-scarred surface of the rocky plateau is covered with a fine mosaic of
greys and browns, split finely by the myriad of dark cracks diving under the
stony surface. Sparse tufts of dry, brown grass whistle in the blowing wind and
gritty dust stirs up with every step. A low ridge cuts of the otherwise flat
landscape to the south, too steep to be climbed. Misshapen piles of broken
granite and weatherworn sandstone heaps dots the ground in all other directions.
name: Rocky Plains

desc:
The tinder dry earth of the rocky plateau is littered with larger rocks and
drifting, stinging sand. Dry, pale lichen splayed out upon the cracked earth
crunches under feet and the wind whistles sharply through the wiry, brown tufts
of grass. West and south, the land slopes steeply upwards into the mountain arms
that enclose this valley.
name: Rocky Plains

desc:
The steep incline of this mountainside is solid and only low plants have found
footing and nourishment to grow here. Even the hardy plains grass seems to have
difficulty pushing their roots into the thin layer of rocky soil. Evidence of
old landslides caused by the lashing force of the harsh elements can be seen in
the wide gullies overhead. To the west, the sheer face of the mountainside bars
any thought of travel in that direction.
name: Rocky Mountainside

desc:
The parched plain is cracked and littered with wind-carved sandstone formations
and loose stones. The only thing that manages to survive here is the hardy
plains grass, shoving up from the hard-packed earth in small, tufted clumps. A
dry wind sears across the plains, whipping the tufts and stirring up eddies of
choking dust. To the south and near west loom the steep and barren cliffs of the
Misty Mountains, enclosing the valley in their arms.
name: Cracked Rocky Plains

desc:
The rock-strewn slope draws up sharply to the southwest, naked to the harsher
elements of wind and rain except for the minimal shelter provided by the sparse
collection of small, dry shrubs that line the lower flanks of the mountains.
Thin, spidery lichen splays out across the uneven ground, giving a pale yellow
tone to the stony landscape.
name: Rocky Mountainside

desc:
The broad plateau is flat and barren, littered with tortured rock formations and
loose scree. A handful of small, twisted thornbushes dot the landscape and a
myriad of web-like cracks fracture the hard ground into a mosaic of clay-hued
grey and dusty earth-tones. To the southwest, you see a formidable wall of
rising mountains, marching away along the horizon.
name: Rocky Plains

desc:
The steep slope of this mountainside is covered with loose rock and scant
vegetation. The coarse grass and dry lichen have a tenuous footing in the thin
layer of rocky soil and the overall landscape appears bleak and barren. The
mighty arms of the southernmost Misty Mountains tower high above you into the
endless sky, sheer and magnificent in their stark beauty.
name: Rocky Mountainside

desc:
The uneven, hard packed earth is covered here with loose gravel and sparse,
brown grass. The mountains rise steeply westwards, blocking any thought of
ground passage in that direction. To the east, the valley broadens, spilling
into the open plains of Nan Curunir.
name: Rocky Plains

desc:
The steep slope of the mountainside climbs vertically to the northwest, with
sheer cliff walls. Not even the lichen and grasses that are so plentiful in the
hillsides around have found stable footing on the sheer rockface.
name: Bare Mountainside

desc:
The rocky terrain is barren and unforgiving here, littered with arrays of
granite boulders and strange sandstone formations. The sheer face of a dark
cliff rises sharply to the north, while the stony landscape continues
uninterrupted in all other directions. Cold winds push and pull at the loose
shrubs and bushes frequently, lifting up sand and litter migrating across the
plain.
name: Below the Dark Crags

desc:
The rocky plateau is flat and barren, covered with the dried husks of pale
lichen and littered with broken heaps of slag and sandstone. Nothing seems to
thrive in this desolate landscape and the hard-packed, stony earth is lined with
deep cracks that seem to grow wider to the east. On the horizon, both north and
south, east and west, the towering Misty Mountains stand prominently.
name: Cracked Rocky Plains

desc:
The rocky plains that stretch away to the northeast come to an abrupt end here,
bordered by a deep crevice in the dry earth. In the darkness far below the
outlines of boulders are visible, along with faint white lines rather like
bones. To the northwest the steep slopes of the mountains are visible, their
massive heights dominating the horizon. A hard wind lashes at anything that
ventures in this harsh terrain, stirring up clouds of dust all about you.
name: Edge of the Crack

desc:
A dark chasm falls away before your feet, plunging deep into the cracked, stony
earth. The rift tears its way north and southwards through the barren plain, the
other side a dozen yards or more away to the east. Nothing much lives on the
rocky plains here, except the rare tufts of dry, brown grass. A few miles to the
west, the towering walls of the Misty Mountains dominate the land.
name: Edge of the Crack

desc:
The rocky plains are sharply severed here as a narrow rift delves deep below the
surface of the plateau at your feet. The climb down does not seem so difficult,
but ascending would be tiring. Across the crevice to the east the barren plains
marches off to the horizon, with only the occasional mound of crumbled rock or
small, withered bush to interrupt the emptiness.
name: Edge of the Crack

desc:
The rocky plateau is flat and barren, dotted with scattered rocks and piles of
boulders. No larger plant life remains here, only the hardy plains grass manages
to thrive, in windswept clumps. The terrain is lined with a tortured mosaic of
fine cracks that dive sharply down into the rocky earth.
name: Boulder-strewn Plains

desc:
The desolate valley plateau stretches onwards in all directions, the monotony
broken by the intricate arrangement of fine cracks decorating the dusty surface
of this forsaken land. Loose piles of broken rock gathered by the wind and small
tufts of brown grass are scattered on the ground.
name: Cracked Rocky Plains

desc:
The dry wasteland stretches out in every direction from here; the forces of
nature having stripped all of the lush growth from the once fertile plain. The
only plant life that manages to remain, are the few tufts of wiry, brown grass
that rustles in the dry wind. Far upon the eastern horizon, the faint line of a
distant road can be made out.
name: Dry, Rocky Wasteland

desc:
The dry wind stirs up eddies of grey dust and gritty sand here, obscuring the
view of the surrounding landscape. Few plants have survived the harsh climate
and weather on this plain; only brown tufts of grass is seen on the ground. A
large outcropping of dark rock can be discerned to the northeast; the granite
formation appears to have been deposited here ages ago by some vast ice slide or
perhaps hurled from afar from some volcanic source long since quieted.
name: Rocky Plains

desc:
The gritty mantle of dust and gravel lies uneasily upon the parched plain at
your feet. The elements have stripped all life from this tortured landscape,
leaving gaping cracks in the dry and rocky soil. To the southeast, a large
granite outcropping stands like a rotund monolith on the horizon.
name: Cracked Rocky Plains

desc:
The barren plain continues onward, decorated sparsely with dusty boulders and
piles of crumbled rock. Even the hardy plains grass has given up on survival
here; deep cracks in the parched earth are evidence of the hardships that this
once great land has suffered. High mountains loom on the distant horizons to the
east and west.
name: Rocky Plains

desc:
What was once a green and fertile plain lies now in dust; the stony ground is
littered with crumbled sandstone and brown clumps of dried grass that whip about
stiffly in the wind. The plain is divided sharply by a system of fine cracks in
the earth that seem to grow larger westwards. Elsewhere, the flat land continues
until the mountains that surround this valley.
name: A Parched Plain

desc:
The dry earth is covered with cracks. The cracks vary in width from a few inches
to a foot, and most of them are parallel, going from the north to the south.
Westwards a larger crevice blocks movement, its dark depths not visible from
here. Barely surviving in the poor earth are a few scraggly bushes, their
withered branches showing little vigour or life.
name: Dry Fissured Ground

desc:
The cracked land breaks up to the west in a wide crevice, a dozen yards in
width. It narrows north and south into a thin fissure, and then disappears
altogether. The area around is fairly flat, with little growth and no larger
plants at all. The dirt is dry and filled with small cracks from the influence
of the sun. The valley continues in all directions, framed by the Misty
Mountains on all sides.
name: Cracked Plains

desc:
Covered in a thin haze of dust that swirls in the wind, the ground here is cut
by tiny fissures as if it had been without water for decades. A large crack
opens up the ground west of here, too wide to cross. The near side is too steep
to descend, overhanging the base of the crevice below. The far side looks to be
less of a challenge, and could be climbed with care.
name: At the Edge of a Fissure

desc:
This barren and rocky plain is lined with thousands of fine, dark cracks that
extend along the surface of the ground. Heaps of broken rock and small boulders
decorate the tortured landscape and only small clumps of wiry, brown grasses
have survived the drought that has plighted the lands. A dry wind throws a small
cloud of dust and gritty sand into the air.
name: Dry, Cracked Flatlands

desc:
The flat surface of this rocky wasteland is dotted with a loose collection of
short, withered bushes. The twisted branches splay out across the dusty terrain
and jerk stiffly in the dry wind that whips now and then across the heath. About
you, the rocky plains continue in all directions, though the ground to the west
seems more barren and lined with dark cracks.
name: A Stony Heath

desc:
A ridge of the mountains, coming from the west, breaks the flat plateau. Where
the ridge ends, a large dike begins, leading eastwards, across the valley. The
dike is deep and wide, preventing travel to the northernmost parts of the
valley. The ground is dry and rocky from the apparent drought and the harsh
climate; only a few clumps of dry, brown grass grows here.
name: Cracked Rocky Plains

desc:
The stark terrain is harsh; the only plant life that seems to survive are the
occasional tufts of brown grass. The hard-packed earth elsewhere is covered in
gritty debris and larger rock formations. Beneath the drifting carpet of
dun-hued dust, you see an intricate network of narrow cracks, growing into a
large yawning chasm to the south. The ground opens up to the north, into a deep
dike preventing access northwards. The bases of the cracks and the dike, far
below, are littered with cracked boulders and often sun-bleached bones.
name: Cracked Rocky Plains

desc:
The rocky ground is littered with piles of crumbling boulders and loose gravel
that grates sharply under foot or hoof. The bones of a small animal, a mule or
pony perhaps, stand out starkly against the brown earth. All around you, except
to the north, the valley plains march on, rising slightly towards the mountains
that surround them. The plains northwards are blocked by a deep dike, preventing
any access.
name: Cracked Rocky Plains

desc:
The rocky table of this barren plateau is dotted with countless rocks and piles
of broken boulders. A fair distance to the northeast, the shadow of a giant tree
breaks the flat lands, against the background of the stark vastness of the Misty
Mountains.
name: Rocky Plains

desc:
The rough terrain dwindles and ends to the east, where it meets the paved
roadway that leads to Isengard. Far to the north, you can see the towering
pinnacle of Orthanc, rising sharply before the mountain peaks. The surrounding
terrain is littered with small stones and boulders, and a few rib-cages of slain
animals.
name: Rough Stony Flats

desc:
This wide and well-maintained roadway leads from north to south, while the
surrounding lands rise steadily northwards as they approach the mountains
encircling the ring of Isengard. A lonely pinnacle rising in its centre, the
towering Orthanc, can be seen against the mountain background. The swift flowing
waters of the Isen lies directly east of the road, a short distance down a soft
bank.
name: Road to Isengard

desc:
The water moves swiftly along the river Isen, on its way south, out of the
valley. A paved road at its western shore and steep mountain walls not far from
the eastern shore, other than that, the plains stretch away across the valley,
unhindered. The river has a fresh, wholesome appearance, and the relentless
force of the water makes a loud noise. The shallow western bank is easily
accessible from the water, but to ascend the eastern bank would mean scrambling
up the wet slippery rocks.
name: River Isen

desc:
The river Isen continues along slowly here, forming the eastern border of the
rocky plains and the road to Isengard. At this point, the river makes a soft
turn in its course from east to south. The water is cold and clear, seeming to
home a great variety of fish.
name: River Isen

desc:
The Numenorean designed military road appears to be in excellent condition. Wide
and paved, the roadway follows the course of the Isen on its western bank,
turning slightly here as the river bends. Not far to the south the road and
river pass through the arms of the mountains and leave the valley.
name: Soft Bend in the Road to Isengard

desc:
The well-travelled and well-tended road to Isengard, follows the swift flow of
the Isen, on its west bank. The military road is wide and paved with great
stones, having been laid with careful design by the Numenorean crafters of old.
Here, the roadway leads from north to south, where it passes through the arms of
the mountains and leaves the valley.
name: Road to Isengard

desc:
The wide, flat surface of the Numenorean-built roadway is in excellent
condition, having been maintained over the centuries by the troops garrisoned
inside the fortress of Isengard. The road runs in a straight line from north to
south, the cold flow of the river Isen on its eastern side.
name: Road to Isengard

desc:
A thin layer of gravel and small stones cover the parched plain like a grey
mantle. Larger rocks lie scattered about, a constant annoyance to an inattentive
traveller, with their sharp edges and uneven surfaces. To the east, the dry
landscape intersects with the paved roadway that runs from Isengard to the Gap
of Rohan.
name: Rocky Plains

desc:
The chaotic arrangement of broken boulders and gravel continues in all
directions along the parched plain. Far away on the horizon, the outline of the
jagged Misty Mountains can be seen. Two arms from the main range to the north
split away, surrounding the valley on its eastern and western sides. A dry wind
stirs up eddies of dust and stinging, gritty sand in the air.
name: Dry, Rocky Plains

desc:
The flat terrain beneath the grey mantle of gritty sand and rock appears to
consist of a table of solid rock. To the southwest, a huge outcropping of
granite can be seen in the middle of the wasteland. To the southeast, you can
just make out the dark line of a road - the road to Isengard.
name: Rocky Plains

desc:
The rocky terrain, barren and dry, seems to come to an end in the east, as it
intersects with the paved road to Isengard. The ground here is littered with
loose gravel and small rocks, and you can barely make out the tracks of men and
horses that have deviated from the roadway.
name: Rocky Plains

desc:
The Numenorean designed roadway seems to have shouldered the centuries well.
Wide and paved with great stones, the road leads from the northeast to the
south, following the flow of the river Isen that lies just to the east. The
valley here is surrounded on all sides but south with the vastness of the
mountains, looming on the near horizon.
name: Road to Isengard

desc:
The rocky mantle of this parched plateau seems to dwindle to the east as it
approaches the road to Isengard. Not far to the northwest, you see a huge grey
block of stone, apparently rammed into the ground by an incalculable force. The
terrain here is littered with loose gravel and broken boulders.
name: Barren Roadside

desc:
A thin, grey layer of stones and gravel would make this terrain a fair path to
travel, even in wet weather, although larger piles of fragmented boulders could
easily trip the inattentive traveller. To the southeast, the brutal landscape
collides with the paved roadway to Isengard.
name: Rocky Plains

desc:
This great roadway was laid down many years ago, by the great craftsmen of
Gondor. The paving stones that form it seem to have been recently reset in
parts, and it appears to be none the worse for its great age. Between the spaces
lining the great stones you see what appears to be fragments of bone, having
changed from grey to brown with time.
name: Road to Isengard

desc:
The rocky riverbank to the east of the roadway is close, as the road to Isengard
lies next to the waters of the Isen. Large, flat slabs of rock rise sharply from
the rippling surface. The road bends slightly away from the Isen southwards,
making its way around a brick watch-tower.
name: Road to Isengard

desc:
The road makes a soft turn from south to east, following the course of the Isen,
which flows swiftly, just east of the pavestones. The mighty Numenorean
architectural design remains in excellent condition; the road is wide and paved,
allowing for swift movement of legions from Isengard in times of war.
name: Road to Isengard

desc:
The wide and paved roadway marches directly north and south here, its stony
surface worn by time but remaining in excellent condition. Afar in the north a
ring of encircling walls running from an outcrop of rock can be seen, and the
black pinnacle of Orthanc standing starkly against the sky in its centre. Just
to the east, down a sharp bank, flows the course of the Isen, its far bank steep
and unclimbable.
name: Road to Isengard

desc:
The wide roadway remains in excellent condition, its Numenorean design having
been maintained over the centuries. It follows the curve of the flowing Isen,
running for many miles to the valley opening far to the south. The road ends
west of here, at the gates of Isengard. A fortress whose outer defence can be
seen not far away to the northwest: a giant ring thrust out from the mountains
themselves, with a central tower like a black spike.
name: Bend in the Road to Isengard

desc:
The wide, paved road runs through the gates of Isengard from the north, leading
away through the valley to the southeast. Immediately south of the gates it
bends away slightly to the east, passing through a gap in the ancient dike
spanning across the valley. The Numenorean-designed road appears to have been
maintained over the years, still well paved with not a blade of grass visible
between the stones.
name: Before the Gates of Isengard

desc:
The high hills are covered with serrated, black basalt rock formations, making
travel much more difficult and dangerous. From the east, you can hear the faint
sound of falling water. To the west lie the stone walls of Isengard, a defensive
ring over a mile from edge to edge. At the ring's centre the tower of Orthanc
can be seen, a tower made of four giant peaks of stone.
name: Serrated Hills

desc:
The small platform slopes very steeply to the east, and the footing consists of
slippery and wet moss, growing on a layer of small pebbles. High above you see a
mountain peak rising sharply into the sky. It is but one of a giant line of
heights that stretches north for countless hundreds of miles.
name: Small Platform

desc:
The rocky terrain is littered with small rocks and broken boulders. A deep dike
lies to the north, worn by wind and weather. On the far side of the old
defensive line lies the ring wall of Isengard, and behind them the tower of
Orthanc, hundreds of feet high. To the east, the paved roadway along the river
Isen lies, while the western horizon is parted in two by a looming, ancient,
dead tree.
name: Rocky Plains

desc:
Low, jagged rock formations jut sharply from the flat surface of the withered
valley. Scattered amongst the piles of broken boulders, black thickets of
tangled thorn-bushes fight for survival in the harsh elements of this once lush
valley. To the north the land gains altitude swiftly, sweeping far up above the
stony plateau in mountainous heights. On the outcrop from one such height lies
the ring of Isengard, a vast fortress. A small house of stone stands against the
wall. To the south the land falls away into an ancient dike, crumbled but still
an obstacle to movement.
name: Bushy and Rocky Plains

desc:
The rocky valley base is scattered with a random arrangement of small, twisted
bushes, the dry wind whistling through their thorny, bare branches. The dusty
earth is littered with countless dark rocks and drifting swathes of gritty, grey
dust. The land gently rises to the north, before being broken by a line of
mountains. South is the remnants of a wall and dike, which once ran for miles
west-east, signifying the outer border of Isengard's control.
name: Bushy Rocky Plains

desc:
The rocky plateau is dry and cracked, littered with the strange formations of
wind-carved sandstone and piles of granite boulders. One such nearby pile has
tumbled, or been forced over, spilling across the earth. Mountains dominate the
entire horizon, rising sheer and encircling the valley in their arms.
name: Rocky Plains

desc:
The dry plain continues in all directions, dotted with loose rocks and the rare
twisted shrub or wiry clump of browned grass. At the base of a large granite
rock, a jumbled collection of sun-bleached bones lie, humanoid in shape, but
whether from man, elf or orc no one can tell. Far to the west a wall of
mountains rising sharply up from the rocky valley can be seen.
name: Rocky Plains

desc:
The land is higher here, compared to the plains in the east. Bushes and smaller
trees grow in the dry earth between the rocks and boulders. A house built of
stone stands firmly down beyond the mild slope to the north while the trees grow
more abundantly west of here.
name: Border of a Forest

desc:
A huge and lonely oak tree once grew here but now is only a black and dried
shadow of its former glory - appearing as a strange and horrific pillar rising
from the stony plain. The ground here is littered with clay-hued boulders dug up
from the old dike to the north. The land appears utterly parched as if from long
periods of drought, although rain must fall here from the mountains frequently.
name: At a Huge, Dead Tree

desc:
The rocky plateau is cracked and barren, littered with the debris from broken
granite boulders and the husks of dead grasses. Two dark figures loom on the
horizon; to the east stands the black shadow of a huge tree, while far to the
northeast you can see the high, spiked peaks of the tower of Orthanc, the centre
of the sheer-walled ring of Isengard. Just north, the crumbled remnants of a
once lengthy dike lie, now little of an obstruction to movement.
name: Rocky Plains

desc:
The barren wasteland marches on in every direction, only broken by a deep and
wide dike dug across the valley just south of here. Sand and gritty brown earth
cling to every available surface and loose stones and gravel lies in heaps
everywhere. Mountains surround the valley completely, ridges and peaks growing
upwards in the distance.
name: Rocky Plains

desc:
Near the edge of the barren, rocky valley, a couple of small, black-barked trees
jut sharply from the hard-packed earth. A dry wind whistle through their
twisted, barren branches. Strangely formed sandstone outcroppings dot the
landscape, and a dense mass of trees occupy the slopes rising towards the
mountains in the west.
name: Some Trees on the Barren Flats

desc:
The entrance of the guardpost consists of a square room, with doors in two of
its four walls. The front door, to the south, is a thick wooden door, as is the
door in the north wall. The house is built of stone and lies directly to the
large wall surrounding Isengard, with the ring wall as the room's eastern wall.
A desk and a bookcase stand towards the eastern wall, while a passage in the
opposite wall leads into another room.
name: Guardpost along the Wall

desc:
The rocky plateau continues, littered with sandstone rock formations and
crumbling boulders. The landscape appears much like the bottom of some vast
ocean, long since drained, leaving only the rocky residue that collects on the
floor. Rising up to the north and west, and slightly further to the east, the
mountain walls towers above.
name: Sandstone Rocks

desc:
In contrast to the barren areas nearby, this part of the valley is rich with
nourished plant life. A large field of wheat and grains grows here, in neat rows
and plenty of water. Sharp sounds of insects can be heard from somewhere within
the field. Boxed in by steep mountain walls, the field continues westwards,
while rocks and bushes occupy the plains to the south.
name: Field of Wheat

desc:
An abundant field of wheat grows at the foot of the mountain. The healthy grain
sways in the chilly wind, like a yellow sea. A flat, featureless mountain wall
stops the field from growing northwards, while neat, dug ditches irrigates the
crop from the southwest. The field continues in all other directions.
name: Field of Wheat

desc:
Piles of rocks and stones lie scattered on the plain, methodically picked up and
removed. Several ditches has been dug, in square formations, to irrigate the
field that will grow here in the coming season. The chilly wind reminds of the
harsh environment and the high mountain peaks tower in the horizon.
name: Field in Development

desc:
Cornered by towering mountains, wheat grows in neat rows in the large field. The
field's healthy grains sway in the cold wind, catching the eye. Small bugs can
be seen in the rich growth between the yellow stalks. A house is built by the
western mountainside, with a pair of large barn-doors facing the field in this
direction.
name: Field of Wheat

desc:
A small barn stands erected at the foot of the mountainside. Three of the walls
are built out of stone, while the west-most wall is the mountain itself. The
ceiling is low and made of thatch. The air is dry and stale, smelling of
livestock. Two large wooden barn doors are built in the eastern wall, and a
small door in the south leads further into the house. Most of the space is used
for storing and drying hay and wheat, but a couple of stalls for cows or horses
are built near the larger doors.
name: A Small Barn

desc:
A simple house, made out of the most abundant building material nearby; stone.
The room is dark; its thatched roof blocks out any light and the windows are
small. Few things occupy the room, a table, benches, and various cupboards. A
small door in the northern wall, an open doorway to the west, leading further
into the house, and a small window livens up the monotonous walls.
name: Inside a Small House

desc:
This is a dark room with stone walls and a thatched roof. The floor is laid with
flat rocks of various sizes. A large stove lights up and occupies the entire
northern wall and a large, sturdy table fills most of the room. The walls are
hung with pots and pans, whisks and utensils.
name: Kitchen

desc:
The mountain walls rise straight up to the northwest and the land slopes quite
steeply eastwards. The woods are sparse and show little new growth in the dry
earth. The harsh climate of sun and wind, rain and snow, clouds and thunder
draws away all nurture from the soil. The standing trees are old, with no or
little greenery.
name: Steeply Sloping Woods

desc:
The plateau continues from the southeast and meets the irrigated fields in the
north. Framed in by steep mountain walls a farm stands against the western
rockface. Tracks of carts and horses show in the dirt and dust, leading to and
from the houses. The farm's main building lies directly to the west.
name: Rocky Plain in Front of a Small House

desc:
The ground slopes quite steeply here, from the mountains in the west, down to
the plateau to the east. A high mountain ridge makes passage to the south
impossible. Trees grow thinly spaced out, savouring the water of a small rivulet
flowing down the slope. The soil looks poor, dry and full of sharp rocks and
large boulders.
name: Thinly Forested Slope

desc:
The square room is filled with beds, in straight rows. The beds are neatly made,
ready for inspection. The bare stone walls appears clean and smooth. A rack for
weapons and several pegs for hanging armour stand near the door in the east
wall. The floor is minutely swept and not a single piece of dust or spider web
can be seen.
name: Sleeping Barracks

desc:
Set in stone, with large window frames in all direction, this watchtower has
been built on top of the guardhouse. The panoramic view from here is impressive,
but somewhat limited by the mountains. The floor is made of wood, and a ladder
connects to the bottom floor, through an open hatch.
name: Watchtower

desc:
The slope up from the plain rises steeply, meeting the mountain wall. Water
pours and drips down along the cliff face, into the ground. The water gathers
into a small rivulet and continues down the slope. Several large and robust
trees grow here, protected from the winds and harsh climate by the high
mountains.
name: On a Narrow Ledge

desc:
In lee of the towering mountain wall, deep in a crevice, a small spring sets
forth. Water is gently flowing from below, clear and cold. Onwards, downwards
and northwards, between rocks and roots, through moss and lichen, below fern and
tree, it flows. The vegetation drinks from the pure water, sheltered by the
mountains, blessed with sun. The place almost shines with purity.
name: Along the Mountain Wall

desc:
At the mountain offshoot, the most narrow point of the valley, a deep dike has
been dug. With walls too steep to scale, the bottom deeper than the tallest of
trolls, wider than any horse can jump, it effectively divides the plains into
two parts: north of it and south of it. Westwards, where the dike meets the
mountain, the wall is less steep, allowing passage to the south.
name: Beginning of a Dike

desc:
The dike continues to the east and west, deep down below the plains. The walls
are high, steep and effectively hinder any passage to the north or south.
Several large boulders lie on the dike's floor, together with heaps of gravel,
stones, broken bits of metal and remains of dead animals. The high walls hide
most of the sky, but parts of it can be seen straight up.
name: Debris at the Bottom of a Dike

desc:
The rocky ceiling of the cavern has collapsed in places, leaving large heaps of
broken granite and sandstone. The cold floor beneath your feet is littered with
stony debris, sand, and bones from various creatures. The only apparent exit
lies back towards the south, where the cavern seems to widen out, allowing more
passage of air.
name: Collapsed Cavern

desc:
The steep walls of the narrow crevice rise up close upon either side, obscuring
the sky overhead from view. To the north the crack comes to a swift and stony
end, while the cliffs gently separate to the south, allowing more light and
fresh air into the dark crevice. Large rocky formations and loose scree sliding
underfoot hinders your movement here. Lying amidst the boulders is a collection
of bare and broken bones.
name: Bottom of the Crack

desc:
The crevice cuts a deep, dark wound through the rocky plains. Widest at this
point, the crack narrows both north and south. Movement is difficult across the
stony floor of the crevice, as loose stones slide underfoot and fallen boulders
lie strewn about. The sandstone cliffs climb high on either side, sheltering
both your passage and any clear view of the surrounding terrain.
name: Bottom of the Crack

desc:
Earth movements eons ago opened up a large crack in the plains. The edges are
worn and the bottom is sloping slightly downwards to the north. Loose sand and
gravel lies between boulders and rocky outcroppings. The crack grows wider and
delves deeper below the surface of the rocky plains to the north. To the south,
the crevice comes to a quick and shallow halt.
name: Bottom of the Crack

desc:
Small and twisted black bushes are scattered all over the rocky landscape here,
providing some protection for what little topsoil is left on arid and brittle
earth. The plains continue to the west and south and the land rises higher in
the mountainous terrain northwards. A small valley lies due north, wedged
between rocks.
name: Thick Bushes

desc:
A high crack in the northern mountain wall has opened up a small confined space,
providing ample protection against the elements. The rock walls protect from
weather and wind, blocking out the sun and rain. A small body of water has
collected on the rock floor, filtering up from below. The water is clear and
cold. The ground is covered with sharp pebbles and rocks.
name: Dip in the Mountains

desc:
The mountainside is rough and rocky; a hasty traveller could get badly injured.
There is a fair amount of ground cover here, with small patches of twisted,
black bushes surviving in the parched land. Mountains rise in majestic
formations all around the large valley, and the ground slopes upwards, towards
the high peaks in the north. Against the backdrop of mountains, a high black
spike stands out, the tower of Isengard - Orthanc, behind the stone walls in the
east. A small guardhouse, made of stone, stands against the wall to the south.
name: Mountainside

desc:
The dry, cracked plains extend in all directions as far as the eye can see. The
plains are divided in two by a huge dike, spanning from the northeast to the
west, across the valley. The dike is deep and wide, forcing any traveller to go
a roundabout way to get across. Sporadic clusters of low, twisted shrubs and
hulking formations of long since cooled slag or weathered sandstone dot the
plains. The dry wind howls across the arid plain and is throwing up sand and
dust into small clouds.
name: Rocky Plains

desc:
The kitchen looks tidy and well kept. A long table lines the east wall and a
collection of primitive cooking tools are laid upon it. There are also a couple
